Okay, here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“Firstly, a large number of neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) have caused significant harm, spreading widely from rural areas to urban centers and entering into various sectors including health, food security, and financial services. Due to these issues, educational initiatives through health campaigns and TB programs have been implemented.  Several individuals have been mobilized for the control of these diseases that have been prevalent (NTDs). The World Health Organization (WHO) is the leading global health body. Its report assesses the effectiveness of efforts to control diseases that are not prioritized (NTDs), which have a high and low prevalence on various indices including mortality rates, disease incidence, clinical cases, health professionals, financial resources, and organizations.” Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the Director-General of WHO, stated this. The report urges a reduction in the number of documents related to disease control and health events reaching rural areas.

NCDs – diseases that cause chronic illness globally.  The number of deaths due to NCDs has been increasing year after year, and the rate has been rising steadily for decades, threatening mortality in many indices.  For example, maternal mortality rates have decreased by up to 11.6% in a single year between 2021 and 2030, aiming to align with the Sustainable Development Goal (SDG). Furthermore, the number of TB cases from 2015 to 2021 represents a significant drop to achieve the 2025 End TB Strategy of the World Health Organization’s Stop TB Partnership. 

---

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àdínkù nínú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú oríṣiríṣi ewu ìlera - bíi lílo tábà, ọtí mímu, rògbòdìyàn, omi tó léwu àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àìdàgbà sókè àwọn ọmọ - ìtẹ̀síwájú náà ò péye bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn ewu kọ̀ọ̀kan bíi bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ sì pọ̀ gan-an. Eyí tó banilẹ́rù ni pé, ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìsanra ń lékún láì sí ààmì ìdápadà ojú ẹsẹ̀. Síwájú sí i, ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ti fà sẹ́yìn ní àfiwé àwọn èrè ṣáájú-2015, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àìsí ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹrẹ nínú àdínkù ìnira ìpèsè owó nítorí iye owó ètò ìlera. Èyí máa mú àdínkù tó lápẹrẹ bá agbára wa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Ìkárí Ìlera Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2030. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 jẹ́ ìrántí pàtàkì pé ìtẹ̀síwájú ò lọ tààrà kò sì ní ìdánilójú," Dr Samira Asma, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Dátà, Ìyànnàná àti Àgbékalẹ̀ fún Ipa WHO kílọ̀. "Láti wà lójú ọ̀nà fún ètò SDG ọdún 2030, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìpinnu àti lápapọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa tó ṣe é wọ̀n ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè." Lára ìjábọ̀ ọdún yìí ni a ti rí fún ìgbà àkọ́kọ́ ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe lórí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìlera, a sì ń fojú sọ́nà pé èyí máa tún ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìjábọ̀ náà láti àsìkò yìí lọ. Fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí àti àwọn agbègbè mìíràn, dátà ní àsìkò, aṣeégbáralé tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì láti tọpa ìlọsíwájú àti láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn òfin ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè èdè àti àgbáyé. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ Olóòtú: Ìjábọ̀ Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ni àkójọpọ̀ ọdọọdún Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) fún àwọn dátà tuntun tí ó wà lórí ìlera àti àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Fún ìbéèrè, kàn sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]
translations.reference: Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]

translations.source: Olùdarí-àpapọ̀ WHO kéde Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Ìlera Lágbàyé Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot ni wọ́n fún ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìfọkànsìn ìgbésí ayé wọn sí ìlera Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé méjì náà láti mọ rírì àfikún aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àgbáyé ní àkókò ìbẹ̀rẹ onípele-gíga fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin. Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Àgbáyé ti Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà, tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, máa lọ sí ọ̀dọ Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot. Àwọn méjèèjì tó gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ yìí jẹ́ onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti olórí ìlera àwùjọ tó yàtọ̀ tí wọ́n jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ nínú ìṣàwárí ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bọ́là ṣíwájú kí wọ́n tó dé ipò aṣíwájú nínú ìlera àgbáyé. Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà bu iyì fún àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé wọn nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum láti Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun, aṣíwájú àti àwọn ìdásí tó lápẹẹrẹ rẹ̀ sí àmójútó àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn bíi ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú mímú àyípadà bá àbájáde ìlera ní Democratic Republic of Congo, ní ẹkùn agbègbè ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, àti ní àwùjọ àgbáyé. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum ni Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ fún National Institute of Biomedical Research ti DRC ní Kinshasa, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n tààrà fún ní University of Kinshasa Medical School àti ààrẹ àkọ́kọ́ fún Congolese Academy of Science. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum wà lára ikọ̀ tí wọ́n ṣàwarí kòkòrò ẹ̀bólà Zaïre ní àkókò tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ ní àkọsílẹ̀ ìtànkálẹ̀ náà ní ọdún 1976, ní Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.reference: WHO Director-General announces Global Health Leaders Awards Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot awarded for lifelong commitment to health WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus will present two lifetime achievement awards to recognize outstanding contributions to advancing global health during the high-level opening session of the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly. The Director-General’s Global Leaders Awards, which were established in 2019, will go to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. Both recipients are distinguished scientists and public health leaders who were closely involved in the discovery of Ebola virus disease before advancing to leadership positions in global health. The award honours their lifetime achievements in public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being recognized for his innovation, leadership and ground-breaking contributions to the management and treatment of diseases such as Ebola virus disease. He has played a critical role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the African region, and the global community. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the DRC’s National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, full Professor of Microbiology at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and the inaugural president of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that discovered the Zaïre ebolavirus during the first recorded outbreak in 1976, in Equateur Province, Central DRC.

**WHO Recognizes Dr. Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot for Their Contributions to Global Health**

The World Health Organization (WHO) has honored Dr. Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot for their contributions to the fight against malaria. Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus presented them with a prestigious award to recognize their significant achievements in the field of global health.

The Global Health Award, established in 2019, has been bestowed upon Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. These esteemed scientists and veteran researchers worked together extensively in the Ebola research project, leading to their advancement to leadership positions in the global scientific community.

This award is a testament to their remarkable contributions to global health.  Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum is highly regarded in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) for his expertise in virology, his distinguished career, and his valuable insights gained through research, including work on the Ebola outbreak. He played a crucial role in combating malaria in the DRC, in Central Africa, and at the international level. Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director of the National Institute of Biomedical Research in the DRC in Kinshasa, the Dean of the University of Kinshasa Medical School, and a corresponding member of the Congolese Academy of Science. He was also among the first researchers working with Ebola virus in Zaire, where he began his work in 1976 in the Equateur Province, Central DRC.

translations.source: Ó tún padà ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìtọ́jú tó ṣiṣẹ́ jùlọ kojú kòkòrò ààrùn náà àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àyẹ̀wo àwọn àjẹsára kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti ṣiṣẹ́ láì sinmi láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá ajẹmọ́-sáyẹ́nsì Democratic Republic of Congo, tí ó ń ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi àwọ̀ ààyè fún ìwádìí ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà, ó sì ti kó ipa pàtàkì pẹ̀lú WHO nínú ìgbógun ti àwọn ààrùn àkóràn. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot láti orílẹ̀-èdè Belgium ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún aṣíwájú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ rẹ̀ nínú ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn KASA àti àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera àgbáyé mìíràn tó ṣe pàtàkì. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìlera àgbáyé tó ṣe pàtàkì bíi UNAIDS, Global Fund, àti àwọn mìíràn tí wọ́n ti mú àyípadà bá àwọn àbájáde ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé. Lábẹ́ ìdarí rẹ̀, UNAIDS di agbẹnusọ àgbà fún ìgbésẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé takò KASA, tó sì tún léwájú àtúntò UN pẹ̀lu ṣíṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ UN 10 ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot tún ti kó ipa nínú ìṣàwárí kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà ní ìbẹ̀rẹ̀, àti kíkápá ìtànkálẹ̀ àkọ́kọ́ tí a mọ̀, tó sì ń kópa nínú àwọn ìwádìí ẹ̀kọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní ààyè ìwádìí. Ó ti jẹ́ alága fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ onípele-gíga, kópa nínú àìmọye àwọn ipò olùbánidámọ́ràn onípele-gíga, tó fi mọ́ COVID-19, ó sì ti gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot ni Olùbánidámọ́ràn Pàtàkì fún Ààrẹ EC, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n nínú Ìlera Àgbáyé àti Olùdarí tẹ́lẹ̀ fún London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. "Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n tayọ wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àpẹẹrẹ ìfọkànsìn ayérayé, agbẹnusọ aláìdáwọ́dúró, ìfọkànsìn fún ìdọ́gba, àti iṣẹ́ àìnímọtara-ẹni-nìkan fún ọmọnìyàn àti ìlera àwọn ènìyàn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ní àkókò tí oríṣiríṣi pàjáwírì, àwọn ìdààmú ìlera, àti rògbòdìyàn wà, a mọ wí pé asíwájú nínú ìlera àwùjọ àti sáyẹ́nsì ṣe pàtàkì púpọ̀ ju bó ṣe yẹ lọ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: He later helped design one of the first effective treatments against the virus and the deployment of experimental Ebola virus disease vaccines. He has worked tirelessly to build the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s scientific capacity, establishing multiple research facilities in the country, and has played key roles with WHO in fighting infectious diseases. Professor Piot from Belgium is being recognized for his extraordinary leadership in the response to the HIV/AIDS epidemic and other significant global health challenges. He has played a vital role in launching major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others that have improved health outcomes around the world. Under his leadership, UNAIDS became the chief advocate for worldwide action against AIDS, also spearheading UN reform by bringing together 10 different UN organizations. Professor Piot also played a part in the earlier discovery of the Ebola virus, and the containment of the first known outbreak, participating in field epidemiological investigations. He has chaired a number of high-level initiatives, served in numerous high-level advisory roles, including on COVID-19, and has received multiple awards. Professor Piot is the Special Advisor to the EC President, a Professor of Global Health and former Director of the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. “These outstanding individuals embody lifelong dedication, relentless advocacy, a commitment to equity, and selfless service of humanity and people’s health,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “In a time of multiple emergencies, health crises, and conflict, we recognize that leadership in public health and science are more important than ever to advance global health.

"WHO kí àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera fún orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali àti àwọn ojú òpó wọn fún àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti abẹ́lé kú oríire fún àwọn àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyìí", Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Lẹ́yìn àṣeyọrí orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali, ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 23 ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO, tó ń mú wa tẹ̀síwájú súnmọ́ àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030. Ní àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali darapọ̀ mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 15 mìíràn tí wọ́n ti gba ìdáláre láti ọ̀dọ WHO fún ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Àwọn náà ni Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo àti Vanuatu. Ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lu lílo Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE WHO Àpapọ orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣàmúlò Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE tí WHO dábàá láti ṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà ni àkójọpọ̀ iṣẹ́-abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àwọn ìnira ìgbẹ̀yin àwọn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú; oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà láti mú àkóràn kúrò; ìmọ́tótó ojú, àti àyípadà agbègbè, pàápàá jùlọ mímú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó, láti mú àdínkù bá ìtànkálẹ̀.
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.” Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission.

WHO urges Benin and Mali to vaccinate against polio as a global health priority, following a partnership with the WHO African Region Office to achieve this. Countries that have received initial WHO funding for polio vaccination include Ghana (in October 2018), Gambia (in December 2021), Togo (in November 2022), and Malawi (in September 2022). “WHO asks that health officials in Benin and Mali, and their counterparts, fulfill their commitments to global and multilateral agreements for these achievements,” said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “Following the vaccination of Benin and Mali, polio eradication efforts are continuing strongly like a key pillar of the WHO’s work for global health initiatives that have been prioritized for 2021-2030. Globally, Benin and Mali have joined the 15 countries that have received funding from WHO for polio vaccination as a global health priority. These include Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu. The Global Polio Eradication Initiative with the WHO’s SAFE Workstream has also adopted the SAFE Workstream of WHO to vaccinate against polio with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE Workstream is a comprehensive framework to identify and respond to polio outbreaks; it uses a combination of battery-operated mosquito nets, reducing the risk of infection, eye drops, and community engagement, to ensure that the virus is contained.

translations.source: Nípasẹ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà azithromycin ni Pfizer máa ń gbékalẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò ìṣẹ́gun tí wọ́n ń ṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà. Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin ti ṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí ìṣẹ́gùn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àwọn tí wọ́n ṣe àmúlò kojú àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì mìíràn (NTDs), lábẹ́ àbùradà Ètò Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD kẹ́ta tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Benin, lẹ́yin sòbìà (ní ọdún 2009) àti ààrun sunrun-sunrun (ní ọdún 2021). Orílẹ̀-èdè Mali ti ṣe àwọn ìwádìí ipa àti ìtọpinpin ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ó sì ti ṣe àgbéjáde àwọn ìdásí láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpèníjà ààbò ní àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àríwá orílẹ̀-èdè náà àti àwọn ìdàrúdápọ̀ òṣèlú-àwùjọ láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD àkọ́kọ́ tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Mali, tí ó ti wá darapọ̀ báyìí mọ́ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àgbáyé fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 47 tí wọ́n ti ṣẹ́gun ó kéré jù NTD kan. "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tó yanilẹ́nu," Dr Ibrahim Socé Fall, Olùdarí Ètò NTD Lágbàyé WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣe àpèjúwe bí èròngbà òṣèlú tó lágbára, ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, ìtọpinpin àti ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ lè ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìṣẹ́gun ààrùn." Ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹẹrẹ ti di ṣíṣe nínú ìgbógun ti ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú láti bíi ọdún mélòó kan sẹ́yìn. Iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìtọ́jú oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ti dínkù pẹ̀lú 84 mílíọ̀nù, láti 189 mílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2014 sí 105 mílíọ̀nù ní oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 41 (títí di oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022) pẹ̀lú àpapọ 125 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó nílò ìdásí kojú ààrùn náà.
translations.reference: Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD. “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease.

Benin has initiated efforts to control schistosomiasis by documenting cases and distributing NTDs (neglected tropical diseases), including a global network for reporting endemic cases. Schistosomiasis is the most prevalent NTD in Benin, following malaria (in 2009) and other recurring cases (in 2021). Mali has also undertaken activities and initiatives for schistosomiasis control, developing tools for verification of vaccination campaigns, with funding provided to regional offices in the country and digital data collection systems to track progress over time. Schistosomiasis is the first NTD prevalent in Mali, which has joined international organizations for 47 countries that have exceeded one NTD case. “These are robust digital tools that are being implemented,” Dr. Ibrahim Socé Fall, Director of the Global NTD Elimination Campaign at WHO, stated. “Benin and Mali are acting as leading digital platforms, with integrated data, leadership, and technological support working together to achieve elimination of diseases.” A specific protocol is now being implemented in the course of schistosomiasis control to track a single year at a time. The number of people reporting azithromycin for schistosomiasis control has increased from 84 million in 2014 to 105 million in December 2022, according to the WHO Regional Office for Africa. A call for attention to the public: Schistosomiasis continues to be addressed through digital tools in 41 countries (as of December 2022) with a total of 125 million people benefiting from interventions in affected areas.”

translations.source: A máa ń rí ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ tí sì luko jùlọ ní Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Central àti South America, Asia, Western Pacific àti Middle East. Àwọn Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ni ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ pẹ̀lú 105 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó kángun séwu, tí ó dúró fún 84% gbogbo ìṣòro ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àgbáyé. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ni àkóràn tó ń fa ìfọ́jú tó ń léwájú jákèjádò àgbáyé. Àkóràn pẹ̀lú batéríà aṣojú ààrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ló ń fa ààrùn náà. Àkóràn máa ń ràn láti ara ẹnìkan sí ẹnìkan nípasẹ àwọn ìka tó ti dọ̀tí, agbé kòkòrò pamọ́ àti àwọn eṣinṣin tí wọ́n ti fi ara kínra pẹ̀lú oje láti ojú tàbí imú ẹni tí ó ti ní ààrùn náà. Àwọn aṣokùnfà ewu àwùjọ fún ìtànkálẹ̀ ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ìmọ́tótó tí kò dára, àwọn ilé elérò púpọ̀, àìní ànfààní tí ó tó sí omi tàbí lílo àwọn ohun èlò ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́. Ní ọdún 1996, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún Ìṣẹ́gun Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2020 (GET2020). Pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tuntun nínú Ìṣọ̀kan náà, WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE ní orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìrólágbára ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè nípasẹ àyẹ̀wo ajẹmọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àmójútó, ìtọpinpin , ìgbéléwọ̀n iṣẹ́-àkànṣe àti àkójọpọ̀ ohun èlò tó ń ṣe àfikún sí ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ náà, tí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ọdún 2020 fọwọ́sí náà nípasẹ̀ ìpinnu rẹ̀ 73(33), mú ọdún 2030 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi déétì àfojúsùn tuntun fún ìṣẹ́gùn àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person. Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“We are seeing a significant increase in preventable diseases in regions where people are dying due to lack of access to healthcare, particularly in Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific, and the Middle East. The Global Health Emergency WHO is leading the effort to prevent these deaths with approximately 105 million people who are dying in these vulnerable regions, a figure that accounts for 84% of global preventable deaths. Preventable diseases are the primary cause of mortality worldwide.  The use of substandard drugs is contributing to this problem.  Drugs are being traded from one person to another through counterfeit products, including fake medications and those falsely labeled with expiration dates.  These counterfeit products are often sold with eye drops or liquids that appear legitimate but contain harmful substances.  Specialized organizations for the fight against preventable diseases are using innovative and reliable methods, including digital tools, laboratory equipment, and educational materials that are proven to be effective. In 1996, WHO conducted the WHO Special Publication for the Global Action Plan for Preventing Diseases in 2020 (GET2020).  Within this publication, WHO is actively working to implement the SAFE strategy in countries and to strengthen national immunization programs through rigorous pharmaceutical quality control, supply chains, healthcare infrastructure, and coordinated efforts to support the global prevention agenda. This initiative, endorsed by the 2020 Global Health Summit, published in the 73(33) issue of the journal in 2030, provides a new timeline for global health goals.”

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I've aimed for a clear and accurate translation while maintaining the document's tone and complexity.
*   Some Yoruba phrases have been rephrased for better flow in English.
*   I've tried to preserve the key information about the rise in preventable diseases, the role of WHO, and the SAFE strategy.

translations.source: 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn Nínú gbogbo àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n bá bí, 1 jẹ́ síwájú àkókò - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ní gbogbo 40 ìṣẹ́jú àáyá, 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó yẹn máa ń kú Iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ní èyíkéyìí ẹkùn agbègbè ní àgbáyé Àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, COVID-19 ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo Ìgbésẹ̀ ni a nílò lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú tó dára fún àwọn ìkókó tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn àti àwọn ẹbí wọn Àpapọ 13.4 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún 2020, tí bíi 1 mílíọ̀nù sì kú látara ìnira ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ tuntun kan tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé gbé jáde lónì. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú bíi 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n tètè bí (ṣíwájú ọ̀sẹ 37 ìlóyún) jákèjádò àgbáyé. Tí wọ́n tètè bí: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) àti Ìpèsè Owó fún Àwọn Ọmọdé ti Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNICEF) gbé jáde pẹ̀lú PMNCH -- ìṣọ̀kan tó tóbi jùlọ ní àgbáyé fún àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ọmọde, àti àwọn ọ̀dọ́, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ lórí "pàjáwírì ìdákẹ́rọ́rọ́" fún ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí wọn ò fiyẹ̀sí-púpọ̀ tipẹ́ nínú ìwọ̀n àti agbára rẹ̀, tí ó ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ìlọsíwájú nínú ìmú àyípadà bá ìlera àti ìwàláyé àwọn ọmọdé. Lára ìjábọ̀ náà ni a ti rí àwọn àkójọpọ̀ láti ọ̀dọ WHO àti UNICEF, tí wọ́n ṣe pẹ̀lú London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, lórí ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Lápapọ̀, ó ṣàwarí rẹ̀ pé iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà ní ẹkùn agbègbè kankan ní àgbáyé láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, pẹ̀lú 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó tí wọ́n tètè bí tó kángun séwu láti ọdún 2010 sí 2020.
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.

Specifically, 1 in every 10 births (a period of 37 years) is globally significant. They have advocated for “ending preventable deaths” related to births, which are disproportionately high in certain countries and hinder educational development for children.

We have received data from WHO and UNICEF, in collaboration with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, regarding the overall number of births.  Overall, it has been noted that the number of births has not decreased in any region globally since 2010, with 152 million births recorded between 2010 and 2020.”

translations.source: Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ti di aṣokùnfà ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń léwájú, tó dúró fún 1 nínú 5 lé gbogbo ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń ṣẹlẹ̀ ṣíwájú ọjọ́-ìbí 5th. Àwọn alájàbọ́ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò lè kojú àwọn ìpadàbọ̀ ìlera títí ayérayé, pẹ̀lú àlékún nínú ìpèníjà ara àti àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìdàgbàsókè tó ṣe é ṣe. Àwọn àlàfo àjàbọ́ nípasẹ̀ ẹkùn agbègbè, ìpawówọlé, ìran Gígùn lórí ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lórí àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ náà ní ọdún 2012, ìjábọ̀ "ọlọ́dún mẹ́wàá" Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tuntun yìí pèsè ìwòye tó kún nípa ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ipa rẹ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lára àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ẹbí, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé. Ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà, ibi tí wọ́n bí àwọn ìkókó sí máa ń sọ bóyá wọn ó ja àjàbọ́. Ìjábọ̀ náà sọ wí pé 1 nínú 10 nìkan nínú àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó lágbára (<28 ọ̀sẹ̀) máa ń ja àjàbọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré, yàtọ̀ sí 9 nínú 10 lé ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Àwọn àlàfo àìṣedéédé tó jọ mọ́ ìran, ẹ̀yà, ìpawówọlé, àti ànfààní sí ìlera ojúlówó máa ń sọ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, ikú, àti ìpèníjà ara tó ṣe é ṣe, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Southern Asia àti ìwọ̀-oọ̀rùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni wọ́n ní iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó pọ̀ jùlọ, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìkókó ṣíwájú àkókò ìbí ní àwọn agbègbè wọ̀nyìí ń kojú ewu ikú tó pọ̀ jùlọ. Lápapọ̀, àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè méjì wọ̀nyìí dúró fún 65% lé àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò káàkiri àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún ṣàfihàn pé àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìbàjẹ́ àwùjọ, COVID-19, àti àlékún nínú owó iná ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive. The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.

The report also highlights that factors such as colorism, eye diseases, and complex financial issues, including COVID-19 and a risk of death due to money, contribute to the risk of death for women and girls throughout the world.”

translations.source: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ ni wọ́n ní ó ń ṣokùnfà 6 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Bíi 1 nínú 10 àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ni wọ́n bí ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 10 tí wọ́n lẹ jùlọ tí rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn ní ṣẹlẹ̀ níbẹ̀, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àlàyé tuntun kan nínú ìjábọ̀ náà. Àwọn ewu ìlera ìyá, bíi ìlóyún ọ̀dọ́ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, ni wọ́n tún ní àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Ẹ̀yí tọ́ka sí ìdí láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ, tó fi mọ́ ìfètòsọ́mọ-bíbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ojúlówó-ńlá nínú oyún àti ní àkókò ìbí. Ètò fún iṣẹ́: àlékún ìdókówò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìpolongo tí àwọn òbí léwájú rẹ̀ Ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn náà tún ti rí ìdagbàsókè nínú ìpolongo àwùjọ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìdènà àbíkú, tí àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn òbí, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera, àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn mìíràn ń léwájú rẹ̀. Jákèjádò àgbáńlá ayé, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ fún àwọn ẹbí oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn ti ń léwájú tí wọ́n ń fún ìpèsè fún ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú tó dára àti àyípadà òfin àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ẹbí mìíràn. Ní ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún Àpérò Ìlera ọmọtuntun àti Ìyá, ní Cape Town, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa láti 8-11 oṣù Èbìbí, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA àti PMNCH ń pè fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí láti mú àyípadà bá ìtọ́jú fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọtuntun àti láti dènà àwọn ewu látara àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: Àlékún àwọn ìdókówò: Ṣíṣe àkójọ àwọn ohun èlò ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti lábẹ́lé láti ṣàmúlò ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun, ṣíṣe àrídájú pé ojúlówó ìtọ́jú ńlá di ṣíṣe ní ìgbà àti ibi tí wọ́n bá ti nílò rẹ̀. Ìṣàmúlò kíákíá: Ṣíṣe àwọn àfojúsùn orílẹ̀-èdè fún ìtẹ̀síwájú nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò àwọn òfin orílẹ̀-èdè tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìtọ́jú ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun.
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.

translations.source: Àfikún yíká àwọn ẹ̀ka: Ṣíṣe àtìlẹyìn fún ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ nípasẹ ìṣẹ̀mí náà; ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ìdókówò ọrọ̀-ajé tó já fáfá, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè-owó-àjọṣe káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka; ríró àwọn ìdáhùn ìfaradà ojú ọjọ́ lágbára kárí ìgbésí-ayé; àti mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìṣàkóso àti ìfaradà àwọn èto pàjáwírì. Ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé: Ìdókówò nínú àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun àti ìwádìí tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àyípadà nínú ìjẹ́ ojúlówó ìtọ́jú àti ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní. Àgbàsọ: Prof. Joy Lawn, Ile-ẹ̀kọ́ fún Ìmọ́tótó àti Ìmọ̀-Ìṣègùn ti London Aṣíwájú-àjọṣe, Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2023) àti Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2012) "Ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí ṣe àfihàn pé èrè àìgbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ni 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Nígbà tí ó kan àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè kan púpọ̀, ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ń dúkokò mọ́ ìlọsíwájú ìlera ní gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè. Ìdókòwò tó pọ̀ nínú ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ọmọtuntun akángun séwu lè dóólà mílíọ̀nù gbé mílíọ̀nù àwọn ẹbí níbi ìbanilọ́kànjẹ́ náà. Ó tún nílò ọ̀pọ iṣẹ́ láti dènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí yóò tún mú àyípadà bá ìlọsíwájú nínú mímú àdínkù bá àbíkú àti ikú ìyá. Lápapọ̀ àwọn ìtọpinpin oníbejì fún ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìtọ́jú máa pèsè àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n ní àlàáfíà láti ṣiṣẹ́ lórí ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwùjọ. Àwọn ìran wa tó ń bọ̀ gbáralé kí gbogbo wa gbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí -- ìdókówò náà lè má kéré ṣùgbọ́n àwọn ère lórí ìdókówò yìí máa ṣe pàtàkì fún gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè "
translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon. While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ itẹ̀síwájú tí gbogbo ayé ti ṣe láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, a ò tíì ní ìlọsíwájú kankan láti mú àdínkù bá iye àwọn ọmọ kékèké tí wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò tàbí ìgbékúrò ewu ikú wọn. Ònkà náà burú jáì. Àkókò ti tó fún wa láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ìyá olóyún àti àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo ọmọ fi ìlera bẹ̀rẹ̀ kí wọ́n sì tayọ ní ayé. Helga Fogstad, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, PMNCH "Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tọ́ka sí ìdí tí a fi gbọ́dọ̀ mú àlékún bá ìdókówò àti ìṣirò fún ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò - aṣokùnfa ikú àwọn ọmọ tó wà lábẹ́ ọdún márùn-ún tó tóbi jùlọ ní àgbáyé. Ìlọsíwájú ti ń lọlẹ̀ fún ìlera ìyá àti ọmọtuntun, bákan náà ni ìdènà àbíkú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ti ń tì í sẹ́yìn lóhùn nípasẹ àpapọ COVID-19, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, àwọn rògbòdìyàn tó ń fẹjú sí i àti àlékún nínú òwó ìná tó burú jáì. "Pẹ̀lú ìṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ ní àjọṣepọ̀ - àwọn ìjọba, àwọn onínúdídùn-ọlọ́rẹ, ẹ̀ka aládàni, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àwọn òbí, àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera ", kí a sì mú àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdèna ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìtọ́jú wá sí gbangba ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdàgbàsókè, ṣíṣe ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ fún àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ẹbí, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé níbi gbogbo. ”Gabriela àti Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: àwọn òbí Jalen Foster, àti olùdásílẹ̀ Jalen’s Gift Foundation "A dá àwùjọ wa sílẹ̀ lẹ́yìn tí a pàdánù ọmọkùnrin wa, Jalen Foster, tí ó jẹ́ àbíkú oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ní 27 ọ̀sẹ̀ nítorí ìjẹyọ batéríà ṣíwájú ìbí kan tí a mọ̀ sí streptococcus irọ̀ B. Pípàdánù ọmọkùnrin wa burú jáì, bẹ́ẹ̀ kò sí àtìlẹyìn kankan nínú àwùjọ náà nígbà náà ni a pinnu láti pèsè ààyè ìtùnú àti ìrètí fún àwọn ẹbí tí wọ́n ti pàdánù ọmọ.
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five. Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.

translations.source: UAE àti WHO ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọkọ̀ òfurufú fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan Orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates (UAE) àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ 30 tọ́ọ́nù àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lónì. Ọkọ̀ òfurufú kan tó gbé àwọn oògùn fún ìtọ́jú ìpalára, àwọn iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì dé sí Port Sudan Airport ní àárọ̀ yìí. Ẹrù náà, tí owó rẹ̀ tó US$ 444 000, ni àkọ́kọ́ tí WHO tí WHO rí fi ránṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀ òfurufú láti ìgbà tí rògbòdìyàn náà ti bẹ́ sílẹ̀. WHO pín àwọn oògùn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn ṣíwájú kí rògbòdìyàn náà tó búrẹ́kẹ́. Wọ́n lo èyí tán lẹ́yìn ọjọ́ díẹ̀ nítorí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n farapa. Ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú náà wáyé gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìtẹ̀síwájú ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdẹ̀run UAE láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan àti láti ṣàfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, pàápàá jùlọ èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń kojú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́. Ìdẹ̀rùn náà tún jẹ́ ìtẹ̀síwájú àfojúsùn ọmọnìyàn UAE ó sì ṣe àfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn tó dá lórí àjọṣepọ̀ ọmọnìyàn àti ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn mìíràn ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ pàjáwírì. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Mínísítà fún Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, sọ wí pé UAE "ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé láti rí i dájú pé àṣeyọrí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò pàtàkì láti fi ìpèsè òògùn àti oúnjẹ pàjáwírì ránṣẹ́ láti lè báni kojú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpè fún gbogbo igun tó wà nínú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ náà láti tètè dáwọ́ ìgbóguntì dúró àti láti mú àdínkù bá ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó ń fa ìjìyà tí ò ṣe é fẹnu sọ fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, UAE dúró lórí ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànwọ́ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní àkókò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict. These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need.
translations.hypothesis:
Here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The UAE and WHO delivered essential medical supplies to Sudan

The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have delivered 30 metric tons of medical supplies to Sudan. A cargo plane transported the supplies to Port Sudan Airport today. This shipment, valued at US$444,000, was facilitated through a partnership between WHO and the cargo plane operator. The WHO is providing the supplies to strengthen healthcare systems as the situation in Sudan deteriorates.  

This initiative is a key priority for one of the UAE’s major development programs, aimed at supporting Sudanese children and establishing a strong partnership with Sudan. Furthermore, the UAE is implementing a youth program to foster cooperation and exchange in areas of health and culture. Dr. Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of International Cooperation, stated that the UAE “is working closely with the World Health Organization to ensure the delivery of medical equipment and supplies to countries in need in Sudan.”  She emphasized the UAE’s commitment to supporting global solidarity and assisting Sudan through its diplomatic efforts, providing humanitarian aid and fostering cooperation with other nations through its ports and trade.

translations.source: UAE ní pàtàkì jùlọ gbájúmọ́ ìpèsè fún àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn aláìsàn, àwọn ọmọdé, àwọn àgbàlagbà, àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ látara rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tó banilọ́kànjẹ́ náà - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú ìdẹ̀rùn yìí máa kojú àwọn àlàfo tó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ nínú ìpèsè oògùn àti oúnjẹ. Iyì ọmọnìyàn tí UAE mú ní ọ̀kùkúdùn túmọ̀ sí wí pé ó ti rí i dájú pé ó ti tẹ̀síwájú láti bá gbogbo àgbáyé sọ̀rọ̀ nípa ìfọkànsìn tó lágbára tí ò sì dúró láti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àlàáfíà, ààbò àti ìdúróṣinṣin, tí kìí ṣe ní ẹkùn agbègbè nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n jákèjádò àgbáyé, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ àti àwọn àwùjọ àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ran àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lọ́wọ́ ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn." Àwọn ohun èlò ìlera náà ni àwọn ohun èlò egbò, ohun èlò iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì tí ó tó, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì láti tètè dé ọ̀dọ 165 000 àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìrànwọ́ ọmọnìyàn gidi gan-an. Àwọn ilé ìwòsàn jákèjádò orílẹ̀-èdè náà jábọ̀ wí pé àwọn ò ní àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì mọ́ àtiwípé àwọn ò ní ànfààní sí àwọn ààyè ìkóògùn pamọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà mọ́ nítorí ìṣòro ààbò. Pẹ̀lú àwọn àtìlẹyìn mìíràn, WHO ń fi àwọn ohun èlo pàjáwírì méjì ránṣẹ́ tí yóò lọ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀-akẹ́rù náà láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n pín àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti láti mú ìtọ́jú dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀. WHO ní tọ́ọ́nù mẹ́tíríkì 30 àwọn ohun èlò mìíràn fún àìsàn ibà àti àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn bíi àtọgbẹ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó lè ṣokùnfà ikú tí wọ́n bá fi sílẹ̀ láì tọ́jú. Àwọn wọ̀nyìí àti bíi 23 000 àpò ẹ̀jẹ̀ ni wọ́n ń múra rẹ̀ sílẹ̀ láàrín ibùdó ohun èlo àgbáyé WHO ní Agbègbè Ọmọnìyàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO ń ṣàmúlò gbogbo ọ̀nà fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ní kíákíá tó bá ṣe é ṣe ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Dr Michael Ryan, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, fún Àwọn Èto Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, sọ wí pé, "Ibùdó Ohun Èlò Àgbáyé WHO ní UAE ti di ilé-iṣẹ́ fún ìdáhùn ìfiránṣẹ́ ojú ẹsẹ̀ fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ alágbára jákèjádò àgbáyé. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún 140 lé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè WHO 6, ibùdó náà ń pèsè ọ̀nà àbáyọ kan fún àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n ń kojú àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera látara ìtànkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àwọn àjálù burúkú bíi ẹ̀kún omi àti ilẹ̀ rírì, àti ipa rògbòdìyàn lórí àwọn ètò ìlera. Àtìlẹ́yìn tí UAE ń pèsè jẹ́ kí WHO lè léwájú àwọn ìdáhùn pàjáwírì ìlera wọ̀nyìí - fífi ìpèsè ìrànwọ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ránṣẹ́ sí àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ níbikíbi àti nígbà-kúgbà tí wọ́n bá nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need. WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed.
translations.hypothesis:
Here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The United Arab Emirates (UAE) offers extensive and valuable support to groups that request assistance for projects initiated in the Republic of Sudan. Specifically, it provides various resources, including medical supplies, equipment, and educational materials, to individuals, children, the elderly, women, and all groups seeking assistance in Sudan.  Furthermore, international experts working in this field contribute to sharing knowledge and best practices through communication and technology.

The UAE’s commitment is demonstrated by its desire to contribute significantly to global health initiatives, promoting stability, development, and resilience, without any regional restrictions. However, the global community, including its partners and organizations, is committed to supporting Sudan and its people.

The country’s leading research institutions report that they do not have access to valuable resources or equipment for public health programs within the country.  With the support of various donors, including WHO, which provides supplementary funds to support 13 specialized centers, the goal is to provide accurate data on the population. WHO is requesting 30 supplementary funds for laboratory tests and treatments that could lead to death, which are being carefully considered.

These, along with approximately 23,000 laboratory samples, are being used to conduct global WHO health initiatives. WHO is collecting data for the delivery of these resources to Sudan, in collaboration with the Ministry of Health in Sudan. Dr. Michael Ryan, Executive Director of WHO Preparedness and Response, stated, “The global health infrastructure of WHO in the UAE has become a key platform for the distribution of emergency health supplies.  The initiative aims to provide 140 countries with a single point of contact for emergency health supplies, including resources for combating infectious diseases, waterborne diseases, and other health challenges.  The UAE’s contribution is such that WHO can utilize these emergency health supplies – a collaborative and effective emergency response mechanism – through communication and technology.”

translations.source: Lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera lágbára, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà UAE, WHO, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ohun èlò láti dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ ní àgbáyé ní àsìkò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ." Ní apá ọ̀dọ tirẹ̀, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Eastern Mediterranean, sọ wí pé, "Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmójútó pẹ̀lú àwọn aláṣẹ ètò ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti fi àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì ránṣẹ́ nípasẹ̀ gbogbo àwọn ojú ọ̀nà tí ó bá wà. Nítorí rògbòdìyàn náà, àwọn ilé ìwòsàn, àwọn ilé ìtàjà oògùn àti ilé ìwòsàn ètò ìlera alábọ́dé ò ní àwọn oògùn pàtàkì, adóólà-ẹ̀mí mọ́. Ìgúnlẹ̀ àfikún tọ́ọ́nu mẹ́tíríkì 30 ti ohun èlò egbò àti iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì máa fún WHO láánfàní láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 àti láti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera tuntun fún àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀. Ibùdó ohun èlò àgbáyé wa ní orílẹ̀-èdè Dubai kó ipa pàtàkì láti ṣe àrídájú ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò ìlera láìsí ìdíwọ́ a sì dúpẹ́ fún àtìlẹyìn tí orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates pèsè láti mú kí ọkọ̀ òfurufú akérò àkọ́kọ́ wa fún àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n nílò gidi gan-an yìí ṣe é ṣe. Àwọn àtìlẹyìn bí irú èyí ni ó máa ń ṣàfihàn àfojúsùn Ẹkùn Agbègbè wa nípa ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, láti ọ̀dọ gbogbo ènìyàn nínú ìgbésẹ̀." Ìràwọ́ oògùn tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé pèsè nípasẹ UAE dúró fún àpapọ 55% nínú gbogbo àwọn ìfiránṣẹ́ oògùn tí wọ́n pèsè fún orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ní ọdún 2022, tó ń ṣàfihàn ipò UAE gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ibùdó pàtàkì fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ ọmọnìyàn ní ilẹ̀-òkèèrè.
translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies. It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“We have mobilized efforts to provide global health services for new initiatives to effectively disseminate information about the UAE, WHO, and various organizations working together to build health facilities to provide care to those who urgently need it internationally, based on their achievements. In that regard, universities, research centers, and international health laboratories are not providing key data, leading to significant errors.  A total of 30 additional health initiatives and related data will be presented to WHO for validation for 13 key universities and to develop new telemedicine practices for those in need. The main health hub is located in Dubai and plays a significant role in reducing the burden of health information, supporting the efforts of the United Arab Emirates to make communication easier between us for essential health issues.  These validations are like a model that showcases our Regional Office’s commitment to health for all people, to all people in the process.” 

The international network of the Global Telecommunications Organization has contributed 55% of all international health communications sent to Sudan in 2022, highlighting the UAE’s significant role in youth support in the region.

translations.source: Àjọṣepọ̀ lábẹ́lé ṣe pàtàkì fún ìlọsíwájú káàkiri àgbáyé fún SDGs tó jẹmọ́ ìlera Ìjábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú tuntun ni wọ́n jọ ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ lónì láti ọwọ́ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó buwọ́lùwé fún Ètò Ìgbésẹ Àgbáyé fún Ìgbé-ayé Àlàáfíà àti Ìgbáyégbádùn fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn (SDG3 GAP). Ìjábọ̀ náà tí wọ́n pe àkòrí rẹ̀ ní "What worked? What didn't? What's next?" ṣàyẹ̀wo ọdún mẹ́rin iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti ọ̀dọ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi tó ń ṣàfihàn àwọn nǹkan tó ti àti èyí tí kò ṣiṣẹ́ láti ró àjọṣepọ̀ àti àtìlẹ́yìn lágbára láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú orílẹ̀-èdè lọ síbi Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá. Ọdún yìí ló pé ìdajì ọdún lọ síbi SDGs, ṣùgbọ́n gbogbo àgbáyé ṣì ń wà lẹ́yìn láti débi àwọn èròngbà àgbáyé náà. Láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, SDG3 GAP ti fi ààyè gba ìdásílẹ̀ àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ tuntun láàrín àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n buwọ́lùwé ní àwọn ààyè pàtàkì bíi ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ àti ètò ìlera alábọ́dé pẹ̀lú àwọn mìíràn. Ó kéré jù àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 67 ni wọ́n kópa nínú ọ̀kan tàbí ọ̀pọ nínú àwọn àkòrí amúṣiṣẹ́ SDG3 GAP. A ti ní ìlọsíwájú pàtàkì, ṣùgbọ́n a ṣì ní ọ̀nà tó jìn láti rìn láti mú àyípadà bá ọ̀nà tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi ń gbà ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè. A gbọ́dọ̀ gbọ́ nǹkan tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ń sọ fún wa kí á sì gbé ìgbésẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọn," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO àti Alága fún Ẹgbẹ́ Àwọn Olórí SDG3 GAP ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Mo máa dúpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ fún àjọṣepọ̀ wọn àti fún àyẹ̀wo-ara-ẹni tòótọ́ tó wà nínú ìjábọ̀ yìí. "Ìjábọ̀ náà pèsè àwọn àbá mẹ́fà pàtàkì láti rí i dájú pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jẹ ànfààní látara àtìlẹyìn tó rọrùn àti láti mọ àwọn ìfọkànsìn tí wọ́n ṣe nínú SDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others. At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's an English translation of the Yoruba document:

“The Global Fund is making a significant contribution to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) that are helping to accelerate their implementation. A new report, titled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?”, examines the first four years of the Global Fund’s work to close the gap between SDG3 (Ending preventable child deaths) and its targets. This report, which assesses the performance of implementing agencies in highlighting what hasn’t worked to inform future partnerships and strategies, aims to help countries reach the Global Fund’s SDGs. This year’s report shows a decline in SDG progress globally, but all countries are still striving to achieve these goals.  Up to 2019, the SDG3 GAP report assessed new collaborative efforts among implementing agencies in countries considered to be performing well using indicators such as financial transfers and data sharing with partners. It covered 67 countries in one or more SDG3 GAP reports. While there has been progress, there is still a need to ensure that implementing agencies are working together effectively to deliver coordinated action for countries. We need to hear from countries what they are telling us so that they can share their information. “Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Steering Group, said this. “I commend the partners for their collaboration and for this comprehensive report. This report highlights four key areas to consider: that countries are making significant progress in coordinated action and to identify the challenges they face in the SDG3 GAP.”

translations.source: Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni: tẹ̀síwájú ìdagbàsókè ìṣẹ̀mi SDG3 GAP fún ìlera pẹ̀lú wíwá ojú ìwòye àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ lórí bí a ṣe ń ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìdáhùn sí àwọn àbá tó fara jọ ọ́; tẹ̀síwájú GAP gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìkànnì fún àjọṣepọ̀; ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè lórí ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ tuntun bíi àwọn ètò ìlera onífaradà ojú ọjọ́; jọ ṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà tuntun ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìlànà àgbékalẹ̀ fún ipa; gba àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ tó pọ̀ wọlé; kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe ìrólágbára fún amóríyá àjọṣepọ̀ nípasẹ adarí òṣèlú, ìdarí ìjọba àti ìpèsè owó láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àjọṣepọ̀ náà. Àwọn kókó ìwádìí ìjábọ̀ náà ni wọ́n máa ṣàfikún rẹ̀ sínú ìjírórò Àpérò SDG Àpéjọ Àpapọ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àti Ìpàdé Onípele-Gíga lórí Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé tó ń bọ̀ ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023. Àwọn oṣù tó ṣíwájú àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí pèsè ànfààní fún ìgbìyànjú àjọṣe fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ oríṣiríṣi láti wá àwọn ọ̀nà lórí bí wọn ó ṣe ṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá láti pèsè ààyè láti ṣàmúlò àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára àti láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lọ síbi àwọn SDG ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá nínú abala kejì àkókò SDG. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú:
translations.reference: These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach; engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:

“These are the points: a high-level SDG3 gap analysis for eye care with the involvement of Partner Countries regarding how we connect at the international level and adhere to standards for those who are starting to grow; a gap analysis as a tool for connection; to facilitate strong connection at the international level for global health initiatives and to pilot new eye care formats from universities; to pilot new models at the international level as a framework for impact; to support organizations with increased capacity; to work with Partner Countries to advocate for increased funding through public opinion, government support, and financial resources to facilitate this connection. These key documents will be submitted through the SDG Platform, the Global Partnership for Vision, and the Big Data Implementation Platform for the Global Eye Health Conference in November 2023.  Dates for the submission of these important documents will be announced soon, with a focus on accelerating the implementation of the Partner Countries and various organizations to identify areas where they can pilot new solutions to pilot strong connections and ensure that the achievement of the eye health SDGs is realized in the near future. translations.source: SDG3 GAP ṣàpèjúwe bí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé ṣe máa ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà ìṣeṣẹ́ tuntun, gígùn lé àwọn àṣeyọrí àjọṣepọ̀ tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀, kí wọ́n sì jọ ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àtìlẹyìn wọn fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tí ó jẹ́ ti orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n sì léwájú rẹ̀. Àwọn tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé fún SDG3 GAP ni Gavi, Ìṣọ̀kan Àjẹsára náà; Ilé-iṣẹ́ Apèsè Owó Àgbáyé fún Àwọn Obìnrin, Àwọn Ọmọdé àti Àwọn Ọ̀dọ́ (GFF); Ìpèsè Owó Àgbáyé láti Gbógun ti KASA, Ikọ́-ife àti àìsàn ibà (Global Fund); Àjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (ILO); Ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àjọṣe lórí KASA (UNAIDS); Ètò Ìdàgbàsókè Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNDP); Ìpèsè Owó Àwọn Ènìyàn Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNFPA); Ìpèsè Owó fún Àwọn Ọmọdé ti Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNICEF); Unitaid; Ilé-iṣẹ́ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé fún Ìdọ́gba Takọ-tabo àti Ìrónilágbára fún àwọn Obìnrin (UN Women);
translations.reference: The SDG3 GAP describes how the 13 signatory agencies will adopt new ways of working, building on existing successful collaborations, and jointly align their support around national plans and strategies that are country-owned and -led. The signatories to the SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); International Labour Organization (ILO); Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); United Nations Development Programme (UNDP); United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF); Unitaid; United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);
