translations.source: Àbájáde ìwádìí tuntun fi hàn pé àwọn ètò ìlera ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí padàbọ̀sípò lẹ́yìn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Lẹ́yìn ọdún mẹ́ta àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àwọn ètò ìlera ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàfihàn àwọn ààmì pàtàkì àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ fìdíhẹẹ́ WHO lórí "Àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19: oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022-oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023". Ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2023, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ kíkojú àdínkù àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣùgbọ́n ṣàfihàn ìdí láti dókówò nínú ìpadàbọ̀sípò àti ìfaradà tó lágbára fún ọjọ́ iwájú. Láàrín àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 139 tí wọ́n fésì sí àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú bíi ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ní òdiwọn. Ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84 níbi tí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ti ṣe é ṣe, ìdá àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tó ní ìdíwọ́ dínkù ní òdiwọ̀n láti 56% ní oṣù Agẹmọ-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020 sí 23% ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022- oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó ń tẹ̀síwájú jẹ́ nítorí àwọn aṣokùnfà ìbéèrè- àti apá-ìdáhùn, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìpele kékeré wíwá-ètò ìlera ní àwọn àwùjọ bákan náà ni àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àwọn ohun èlò ìtọ́jú mìíràn bíi àwọn ilé ìwòsàn tó ṣí sílẹ̀ tàbí àwọn ìfipamọ́ oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò tí ó wà. "Ó jẹ́ ìròyìn ayọ̀ pé àwọn ètò ìlera ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣe ìdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì fún mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣe àfẹ́rí wọn ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà," Dr Rudi Eggers, Olùdarí fún Ìpèsè iṣẹ́ Ìlera Aláfibọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ṣùgbọ́n a nílò láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tẹ̀síwájú láti dí àlàfo yìí láti ṣe àdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìlera tó múra sílẹ̀ tó sì ní àfaradà gidi gan-an fún ọjọ́ iwájú". Àwọn ààmì ìdánimọ̀ àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò Nínú ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè díẹ̀ jábọ̀ mí mọ̀ọ́mọ̀ ṣe ìgbéléwọ̀n ànfààní padà jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ìkànnì fún ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ tó ṣe pàtàkì láti ìjábọ̀ ọdún 2020-2021, tó ń ṣàfihàn ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì láti padà sí àwọn ìpele ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ ṣíwájú-àjàkálẹ̀-ààrùn àti ìṣiṣẹ́ ètò tó fẹjú.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023. Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis:
Here’s an English translation of the Yoruba document:

“Recently, the World Health Organization (WHO) has been advocating for a “Step-Up” strategy for pandemic preparedness for essential health products in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic. Over the past three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, countries have begun to share key priority health products. Following a WHO advisory on “Step-Up for Pandemic Preparedness for Essential Health Products in the Context of the COVID-19 Pandemic: July 2022 – December 2023,” countries began to submit data in a coordinated manner regarding the use of essential health products.

As of early 2023, countries have pledged to share data in a coordinated manner regarding health products, although sharing has been limited due to significant costs and challenges for national health systems. Of the 139 countries to which the “Step-Up” strategy was recommended by WHO, countries have pledged to increase the use of health products in a ratio of one in two. In 84 countries, the rate of utilization has decreased from 56% in March 2020 to 23% in July 2023.

The increased use of health products is due to increased demand and supply chain issues, which are being felt by healthcare professionals and various stakeholders such as educational institutions and global organizations and logistics companies. “It is important to note that health products have enabled many countries to implement priority health products for millions of people during this pandemic,” said Dr. Rudi Eggers, Director of Global Health Security and Environment at WHO. “However, there is a need to ensure that all countries implement this strategy to effectively implement the use of health products, and to scale up training programs developed to strengthen pandemic preparedness and reduce the impact on national health systems.”

In this latest report, several countries have pledged to provide full support for the implementation of coordinated work and essential health services that are important to respond to the 2020-2021 pandemic, and are sharing a significant reduction to improve the performance of essential health services and pandemic response.”

translations.source: Ní ìparí ọdún 2022, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àwọn ààmì díẹ̀ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ìpèsè iṣẹ́, tó fi mọ́ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, ìbímọ, ìyá, ọmọtuntun, ọmọ àti ọ̀dọ́; aṣaralóore; àjẹsára; àwọn ààrùn tó máa ń ràn (tó fi mọ́ àìsàn ibà, KASA TB, àti àwọn ààrùn látara ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn); àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì; àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn; àmójútó ọpọlọpọ, àwọn ààrùn ajẹmọ́-ọpọlọ àti ìlòkulò oògùn; ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti darúgbó; àti ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àti/tàbí ìbádọ́gba. Iye àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń jábọ̀ ìdíwọ́ fún ètò orílẹ̀-èdè wọn dínkù láti bíi ìdajì (29 nínú 59 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) sí bíi ìdámẹ́rin (18 nínú 66 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ń fèsì) láàrín ọdún tí ó kọjá. Pẹ̀lú àwọn ààmì ìpadàbọ̀sípò, àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ṣì ń wáyé jákèjádò àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àti àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti jákèjádò ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ààyè ìṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn ààyè iṣẹ́ ìtọpinpin. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tún kojú àlékún àṣepatì iṣẹ́ - ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà níní ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún àyẹ̀wò, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn - tí ó lè yọrí sí nǹkan tí ò dára pẹ̀lu bí àwọn ènìyàn bá ṣe ń ní ìdíwọ́ ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú lásìkò. Ìpadàbọ̀sípò àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ṣe pàtàkì nítorí àwọn ìdíwọ́ - tó fi mọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìpolongo ìlera, ìdènà ààrùn, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn, ìtọ́jú, ìtúnirọ àti ẹ̀rọ̀ - lè ṣe àkóbá tó burú púpò fún ìlera lára àwọn ènìyàn àti ìpele ẹnìkan ju àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn fúnra rẹ̀ lọ, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu. Ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì Nínú ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì mìíràn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò àti ìṣípòrọpò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera. Bíi 80-90% àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n ti ṣe àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára COVID-19, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti àmójútó ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pátápátá bákan náà ni ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ lẹ́yin COVID-19 sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́. Síbẹ̀, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè (80% nínú 83 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) jábọ̀ pé ó kéré jù ìdíwọ́ kan fún ṣíṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ COVID-19 tó ṣe pàtàkì (b.a. àyẹ̀wò fún ìdámọ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, ìtọ́jú, àjẹsára àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìdáàbòbò àdáni - PPE), pẹ̀lú Ìṣòro iye àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àìsí ìpèsè owó tó dúró fún àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó wọ́pọ̀ júlọ. A nílò ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàmúlò nǹkan tí wọ́n kọ́ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tó fi mọ́ nípa ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń ṣe àkóbá fún àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.
translations.hypothesis:
Here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“In the year 2022, numerous countries have submitted data to support the implementation of digital trade registries, for the purpose of trade facilitation, customs, taxation, youth, children, and dependents;  losses;  tariffs;  goods that are subject to taxes (including electronic invoices, KASA TB, and standard trade registry documents);  taxable assets that have been acquired;  assets that have not been acquired;  various amounts,  trade-related amounts and digital services;  and  legal documents for individuals who have applied. The number of countries providing information for the anniversary of their country is increasing to meet the criteria (29 out of 59 countries providing data) as a percentage (18 out of 66 countries providing data) for the year in which it was published.  With regard to digital trade registry implementation, information on trade services is increasingly providing countries with regional and global levels, and also providing comprehensive data on various trade activities and digital services. Countries are also submitting service tax reports – in many cases, within the trade sector for training,  reporting of taxes, and legal documents that may be submitted.  Digital trade registry implementation is important due to the information – for customs clearance,  customs reporting, training,  legal documents, and  monitoring – can effectively address the needs of individuals and levels of organization more than the amount of tax collected.  The implementation of COVID-19 trade services within the trade registry sector is also important, with a significant proportion of countries having made submissions of COVID-19 trade services through a digital platform.  Approximately 80-90% of countries have submitted data for the implementation of a COVID-19 financial data exchange (e.g., reporting for customs,  taxation,  trade-related amounts and COVID-19 launch data).  Furthermore, many countries (80% out of 83 countries providing data) have submitted a single report for the implementation of COVID-19 business data exchange (e.g., reporting for customs,  taxation,  trade-related amounts and  basic protective equipment - PPE), along with the development of trade and financial resources for relevant data.  We have initiated a coordinated effort for implementation,  innovation, and  trade and digital services.  Several countries have begun to sample data from COVID-19 tax reports,  as documented in the official tax reports.  This includes the implementation of various trade services in the field of natural sciences that are being tested through the trade sector. ”

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for a clear and accurate translation while preserving the original document's tone and complexity.
*   Some Yoruba phrases have been translated more literally to maintain the original meaning.
*   I've used terms like "digital trade registry" to represent the concept, as a precise equivalent in English might not fully capture the nuance.

Would you like me to elaborate on any specific part of the translation, or perhaps provide a more simplified version?

translations.source: Nínú èyí ni ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà ìṣẹ̀gùn orí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, ìpolongo ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́-ilé tàbí àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-ara-ẹni, àwọn ìlànà fún ríró wíwà níkálẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lágbára, àwọn ìkápá àti àwọn ètò àtìlẹ́yìn, àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun fún rírà àti ṣíṣe ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ, àti àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn olùpèsè ní ẹ̀ka aládàni. Ìdá mẹ́ta nínú mẹ́rin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àfikún ìpèsè owó fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ ètò ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣàlàyé ìdí fún àtìlẹ́yìn WHO láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà tí ó kù ní ọ̀gangan ipò COVID-19 àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ, ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú ìrólágbára àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá àmójútó fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé -- àwọn àwòṣe ìtọ́jú tó dọ́gba, ìṣèjọba, òfin àti ètò ṣíṣe àti ṣíṣe ètò owó àti ìpèsè owó. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún àwọn olóòtú: Nínú abala kẹrin ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 222, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè ni wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti fèsì sí ìwádìí orí-ibùdó-ìtàkùn tó jẹ́ ojúlówó láàrín oṣù Bẹlu àti oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Ìwádìí kẹrin ṣe ìtẹ̀lé lórí àwọn ìwádìí ìsinmi àtẹ̀yìnwá ọdún 2020 àti 2021 WHO: Abala 1 (oṣù Èbìbí-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020), Abala 2 (oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ-Èrenà ọdún 2021), àti Abala 3 (oṣù Bẹlu-Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2021) tí ó ṣàfihàn bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ṣe ń kóbá ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì àti bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń gbé ìgbésẹ̀. Nígbà tí ìwádìí ìsinmi ní àwọn kùdìẹ̀-kudiẹ bíi ìjábọ̀ aṣègbè àti ṣíṣe aṣojú, agbára ìgbìyànjú yìí ni wí pé ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn kíákíá. Ọ̀rọ̀ "orílẹ̀-èdè" ni a gbọ́dọ̀ ní òye rẹ̀ pé ó ní àfikún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè. Wọ́n parí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ náà pẹ̀lú ìdáhùn láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84, àwọn agbègbè àti ààyè tí wọ́n dáhùn sí ó kéré jù abala ìwádìí kan nínú gbogbo àwọn àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí ìsinmi mẹ́rin náà.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action. While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.

It also addresses global health financing, with a focus on the World Health Organization’s (WHO) contributions to combating the COVID-19 pandemic and related issues, often in collaboration with healthcare workers, developing digital health tools, and financial mechanisms.

A significant portion of the document details the WHO’s financial contributions, covering 22 countries, regions, and entities, distributed over three periods: October 2020, September 2021, and July 2021. The document also addresses the decline in the effectiveness of certain interventions and the need for adjustments. The document concludes with a commitment to distributing this information alongside a framework for managing the financial contributions of the WHO, referencing specific periods and the impact on the availability of medical equipment and the distribution of resources.”

translations.source: Àlàyé ti ayẹyẹ fún àjọ̀dún 50th HRP: Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland Láti ṣàlàyé ìdùnnú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Òfin àti Ìdarí (PCC) tí wọ́n ṣe ayẹyẹ àjọ̀dún 50th HRP àti àwọn àṣeyọrí rẹ̀ lóríṣiríṣi, Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland ka ìwé ìfi ẹ̀mí ìmoore hàn àti ìkíni kú orí-ire níbi ìpàdé 36th HRP PPC. Ìran wa ti rí ìlọsíwájú tó pọ̀ nínú ìmọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ju gbogbo àwọn ìran tí wọ́n ti gbawájú lápapọ̀ lọ a sì ti rí oríṣiríṣi àyípadà ọgbọ́n, ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, ìṣègùn àti àwùjọ tí àyípadà àwọn ohun adèna oyún léwájú rẹ̀ tí àwọn ìlọsíwájú nínú ìlera ìbímọ, àwọn ìlera ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun náà sì tẹ̀lé e bákan náà ni àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àti ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àwọn ìpèníjà tó pọ̀ ní ẹ̀ka SRHR ń tẹ̀síwájú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni, ó ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan tún wà nínú ìbéèrè lónì. A gbóríyìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alátílẹyìn lórí àìmọye àṣeyọrí títí di òní a sì mọ rírì àwọn ìgbéṣẹ̀ ńlá tí wọ́n ti gbé ní àkókò ìdajì ọgọ́run ọdún nínú ìpèsè ẹ̀rí tó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn kíkún àti ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́. Yóò wùn wá láti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìsọ̀rí àṣeyọrí pàtàkì mẹ́ta: Akọ́kọ́, ìwádìí ojúlówó ńlá tí HRP ṣe ní ipa pàtàkì lórí òfin ìlera àwujọ, àwọn òdiwọ̀n, àti ìfikọ́ra jákèjádò àgbáyé tó ń kojú àwọn ìdí fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn ènìyàn tó ń yí padà. Ẹ̀kejì, HRP, ní pàtàkì jùlọ nípasẹ ìṣọ̀kan HRP, ti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìwádìí lábẹ́lé àti káàkiri àgbáyé, ó sì ti, jẹ́ kí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn oníwádìí ní àwọn ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe ìwádìí tí ó ń rí i dájú pé ìwádìí SRHR ojúlówó-ńlá lè tẹ̀síwájú, àti pẹ̀lu bẹ́ẹ̀ náà lékún, lọ sí ọjọ́ iwájú. Ẹ̀kẹ́ta, ní ilé ayé tí ìdààmú, rògbòdìyàn àti àlékún àtakò fún ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo ń dojúkọ, HRP ti ṣe ìgbédìde àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin àti àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu. HRP ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi irinṣẹ́ pàtó láàrín ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé fún ìwádìí nínú ìbímọ ènìyàn ó sì ń tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ olórí aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ nínú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights. We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“The 50th Anniversary of the HRP (Health Rights Programme) was celebrated. The Committee for Women and Children (PCC), responsible for organizing the 50th Anniversary of the HRP and its various related events, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, presented a proposal for increased funding and support for the 36th HRP PPC.

We have observed a significant increase in research and expertise in various fields, including science, technology, engineering, and specialized knowledge, with numerous innovative ideas being presented. These innovations within the field of advocacy, scientific research, and related areas have yielded valuable and relevant insights. 

Furthermore, the endorsement of SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health Rights) issues is being increasingly recognized.  Therefore, we strongly urge the HRP staff, representatives, and volunteers to be vigilant against inappropriate documents that have been circulating for many years and are being used to undermine the credibility of the program and its achievements. 

Specifically, we advocate for the recognition of three key areas: First, the positive impact of HRP on public health policies, healthcare professionals, and the global advancement of SRHR. Second, HRP’s significant contribution through collaborative efforts with various organizations, providing expertise in SRHR advocacy, and subsequently disseminating this knowledge internationally. Third, the importance of addressing the challenges faced by women and girls, and individuals seeking sexual and reproductive health services.

We also encourage the implementation of a comprehensive strategy to combat misinformation and harmful narratives. HRP has prioritized the recognition of important issues related to women and girls, and individuals seeking sexual and reproductive health services. 

Finally, HRP continues to operate effectively in partnership with the Global Inter-Parliamentary Union, and is committed to serving as a key voice in global advocacy and governance.”

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I’ve tried to maintain the formal tone of the original document.
*   I’ve included the acronyms (HRP, PCC, PPC, SRHR) in parentheses for clarity.
*   I’ve aimed for accuracy while ensuring the English is clear and understandable.

translations.source: HRP àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ ti mú wa súnmọ́ ìlérí Àpérò Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ti ọdún 1994 lórí Iye àwọn Ènìyàn àti Ìdàgbàsókè àti ìpe rẹ̀ láti fi àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin, sí àárín gbùngbùn ìdagbàsókè alálòpẹ́. A fẹ́ fi ẹ̀mí ìmoore wa hàn àti ìdúpẹ́ fún ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀, àwọn onígbọ̀wọ́-àjọṣe rẹ̀ àti ojú òpó àgbáyé ti àwọn ààyè àjọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ìgbìyànjú rẹ̀ yọrí sí mímú àyípadà bá àwọn obìnrin, ọkùnrin, ọmọdé, ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn oríṣiríṣi àwùjọ tí HRP ní àfọkànsìn láti ṣiṣẹ́ fún. Ní orúkọ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ PCC, ẹ jẹ́ kí á tún ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ wa pẹ̀lu yín láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àṣeyọrí HRP, lórí èyí tó ṣe wí pé àṣeyọrí Èròngbà ọdún 2030 gbára lé.
translations.reference: HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.

translations.source: WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti múra sílẹ̀ dáadáa fún àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn lọ́jọ́ iwájú, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun lónì tí yóò pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ṣíṣe ètò aláfibọ̀ fún ìdáhùn sí èyíkéyìí afàìsàn èémí bíi ọ̀fìnkì tàbí kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà. Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tuntun náà àti Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó Ń Jẹyọ tàbí PRET, ṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà ìgbàlódé fún ẹ̀kọ́ àjọkọ́ àti iṣẹ́ àjùmọ̀ṣe tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àwùjọ mìíràn láìpẹ́ yìí. Nípasẹ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà, WHO máa lo àkóràn kan láti tọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè sọ́na nínú ètò ṣíṣe fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, pẹ̀lú bó ṣe jẹ́ wí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìkápá àti ìkáátò ni ó wọ́pọ̀ láàrín àwọn ìpín afàìsàn. PRET dáhùn ìpè fún ìtọ́nisọ́nà onímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe ìpolongo àti ìrólágbára ìmúrasílẹ̀ aláfibọ̀ àti ìdáhùn, gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọ́n ṣe là á kalẹ̀ nínú àwọn ìfẹnukò Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera mìíràn ti fi hàn pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nílò láti ní ìgbaradì fún iṣẹ́ láti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn, pẹ̀lú àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ àkànṣe lọ́wọ́ wọn àti ìdarí àti àjọṣepọ̀ tó dára pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀ka mìíràn bíi iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀. "Àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmúrasílẹ̀, ìdènà, àti ìdáhùn ò gbọdọ̀ jẹ́ agbègbè ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera nìkan," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera ṣe ní ipa káàkiri ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀ka, bẹ́ẹ̀ náà ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn lọ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka, àwọn iṣẹ́ àti àwọn afàìsàn. Ó ṣe pàtàkì, bákan náà, pé kí ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìdọ́gba jẹ́ àfojúsùn àwọn ìgbìyànjú wa, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n dẹ́yẹ sí tí wọ́n sì kángun séwu jùlọ." PRET jẹ́ àyípadà kan nínú ìgbésẹ̀ WHO fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò lẹ́nsì ìlànà àkóràn, yàtọ̀ sí gbígbájú mọ́ àwọn ààrùn kan pàtó.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases.

These new technologies and innovative pharmaceutical solutions for the conservation of wildlife or PRET, are exploring industries and strategies for training and capacity building for advanced education and specialized services that will be held in the COVID-19 and various health laboratories.

Through this innovation, WHO is implementing a system to inform countries about the operation of vaccines, with the understanding that many documents and protocols are common among public health agencies. PRET has requested information and technical advice for the operation of digital and pharmaceutical surveillance systems and the implementation of national public health and surveillance, as it does in international meetings. 

COVID-19 and various health laboratories have concluded that countries are able to provide services to deliver vaccines effectively, with digital and global initiatives to support them and appropriate and reliable partnerships with various sectors such as education. “Pharmaceutical services, data, and surveillance should not be considered separate sectors,” said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “As health laboratories play a crucial role in many sectors, so too do pharmaceutical and surveillance systems in sectors, services, and agencies. It does not matter, however, that these systems are integrated into our partnerships, with great attention to the people who are aware of and request additional support.” PRET is a partner in WHO’s initiative for vaccines through a pilot program for a comprehensive strategy, aiming to specifically identify certain vaccines.

translations.source: WHO máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àgbéjáde ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí àwọn ààrùn kọ̀ọ̀kan bó ṣe yẹ. Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà máa wáyé ní ọjọ́ tó gbẹ́yìn nínú ìpàdé náà pẹ̀lú 120 lé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣojú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, àti Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba WHO, láti wá ojú ọ̀nà fún ìtẹ̀síwájú fún PRET, tó fi mọ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ẹ̀kọ́ àkọ́kọ́ Ìpilèṣẹ PRET gbájúmọ́ àwọn afàìsàn èémí, tó fi mọ́ ọ̀fìnkì, kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà, àti àwọn kòkòrò ajẹmọ́-hóró èémí. Pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ àti ewu ọ̀fìnkì látara ẹyẹ tó ṣe é ṣe, ẹ̀kọ́ yìí máa jẹ́ kí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìsọdituntun àwọn ìgbìyànjú ètò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn èémí wọn gidi gan-an láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n ní ìkápá àti ìkáátò tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ nílẹ̀. Ìlànà kan ń lọ lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìpín afàìsàn tí ó kàn, bíi àwọn ẹ̀fọn, tí wọn yóò kojú lábẹ́ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ yìí. Èyí máa tẹ̀lé àwọn kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nípasẹ àwọn àbá mẹ́wàá láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà (HEPR) lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ PRET náà ṣe àfihàn ìgbà ọ̀tun fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ó sì dúró fún àyípadà nínú àwọn ojúlówó iṣẹ́ WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún I, ìdènà, àti ìkápá àti ìkáátò ìdáhùn wọn lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ̀ náà tún lè ṣiṣẹ́ láti sọ àwọn èròngbà àti ìpèsè Ìsọ̀kan Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn di ṣíṣe, tí àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ń jíròrò lé lórí lọ́wọ́. Lẹ́yìn àwọn àfikún àti ìjírórò láàrín àwọn akópa nínú ìpàdé náà, WHO pe àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ sí ìpè láti ṣiṣẹ́ gbogbo àgbáyé yìí láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́
translations.reference: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative. This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action

Furthermore, the project will provide a platform for the dissemination of essential medicines, including data, regulations, and standards (HEPR – likely an acronym for a specific health program).

This PRET project will establish a timeline for the development of essential medicines and will also support collaboration among WHO offices to ensure that WHO Member States receive adequate data, regulations, and standards. The project can also be used to share best practices and experiences in global health.

Following the consultations and collaborations with stakeholders, WHO has called for all stakeholders to work together to achieve a global standard for the production of essential medicines.  A call to action...

translations.source: WHO, Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn tó ń jẹyọ ní orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé lágbára. A mọ rírì ipa àwọn àwùjọ àti gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀ka, àti ìdí fún ìtẹ̀síwájú ní ìṣọ̀kan àti alálòpẹ́ láti fi òpin sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ ẹ̀rù àti ìdẹ́yẹsí gbogbo ìgbà tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ nínú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. A rántí àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ láti inú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àtẹ̀yìnwá, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn tí afàìsàn èémí ń fà. Àwọn afàìsàn wọ̀nyìí ti wọn ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ ewu àgbáyé tó lápẹẹrẹ pẹ̀lú agbára láti fa ààrùn tó pọ̀ àti ikú, pọ̀jù fún àwọn ètò ìlera, da ọrọ̀ ajé àgbáyé rú, kí ó sì mú kí àìṣedéédé ó burú síi, èyí tí ó wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dènà àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti ètò ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Èyí jẹ́ Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ láti mú kí ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu tó ń jẹyọ jákèjádò àgbáyé yá. Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ gbára lé ètò ṣíṣe tó tóbi àti ìgbésẹ̀ tó ní àmójútó. Ìfọkànsìn àjọni lórí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí máa jẹ́ kí á ní ìlọsíwájú tí yóò bá fi di oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2025 gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọn ó ṣe ṣàpèjúwe rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àmójútó (Ìgbaradì àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ṣe ìsọdituntun àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ tí ó fọwọ́ sí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n sì ti gbèro kíkọ́ ẹ̀kọ́ látara àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àtẹ̀yìnwá. Mímọ àwọn ewu tí àwọn afàìsàn èémí lè fà, ṣíṣe ètò fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí pẹ̀lú àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ tí wọ́n di ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nínú Ẹ̀kọ́ PRET #1: Ṣíṣe ètò fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí jẹ́ kàn-án-pá
translations.reference: WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally. Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority

translations.source: Àlékún nínú àjọṣepọ̀ láàrín àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ nínú ṣíṣe ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìdarí àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ tó létò. Lára èyí ni ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò adọ́gba; ṣíṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ àjọṣe; àti pínpín ìròyìn lórí àwọn ìṣesí tó dára, àwọn ìpèníjà, àti àwọn ànfààní. Ṣe ìyàsọ́tọ̀ àwọn ìdókówò, ìpèsè owó àti àmójútó fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn pàtó lórí kíkojú àwọn àlàfo tí wọ́n rí ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé tó kọjá. Ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti ṣàpèjúwe nǹkan tí ó ṣe é ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn òṣèlú, ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìpèsè owó. Ní àárín gbùngbùn iṣẹ́ yìí ni láti rí i dájú pé ìdọ́gba ti wà nílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn tó ń bọ̀ lápapọ̀. A nílò ìgbésẹ̀-gbogbo-àwùjọ láti jẹ́ kí ìlọsíwájú tí wọ́n là kalẹ̀ nínú Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí di ṣíṣe. Nítorí náà ṣíṣe àmúlò gbọ́dọ̀ ró ìfaradà àwọn àwùjọ lágbára; ṣe ìtẹ̀síwájú, àlòpẹ́, kí ó sì gùn lé àwọn ètò ìlànà-iṣẹ́; kí ó sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ìkápá tó gbòòrò fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí pàjáwírì. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí ni wọ́n gbé jáde níbi Ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ìpàdé Àgbáyé fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí Ọjọ́ Iwájú tó wáyé ní 24-26 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023 ní Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding. At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.

translations.source: WHO pè fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ tí wọ́n dábàá láti kojú àìsàn ibà Láti sààmì Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ń fi ìpè síta fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí tuntun àti àwọn tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ láti dóólà àwọn ẹ̀mí kúrò lọ́wọ́ àìsàn ibà. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ tó 1.5 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìlera àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà ní orílẹ̀-èdè Ghana, kenya àti Malawi ni wọ́n ti gba oògùn àkọ́kọ́ fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà àkọ́kọ́ báyìí, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), ọpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ ètò alábẹ ṣékélé kan tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí WHO ṣàkóso rẹ̀. Àjẹsára alábẹ ṣékélé àìsàn ibà náà, tí wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, ń mú àlékún bá ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìdènà àìsàn ibà fún àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ ó sì ń dóólà ẹ̀mí. Tí wọ́n bá ṣàmúlò rẹ̀ lọ́nà tó gbòòrò, WHO ṣírò pé àjẹsára àìsàn ibà lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. A ní àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dín àìsàn ibà kù, àpapọ àwọn ìdásí tí ó ní ìdènà àwọn alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn oògùn ìdèna àìsàn, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìtọ́jú," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tí ò léwu tó sì ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú, tí ó lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Pẹ̀lú ìdókówò alálòpẹ́ àti fífẹ àwọn ìgbìyànjú lójú láti dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ, ìṣẹ́gun àìsàn ibà ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó. "Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà ọdún 2023 ni wọ́n ń sààmì lábẹ́ àkòrí “Àkókò láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àìsàn ibà òdo: dókówò, ní ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, ṣàmúlò”. Nínú àkòrí yìí, WHO ń pè fún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa láti dènà, ṣe àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ àti ìtọ́jú àìsàn ibà, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dẹ́yẹ sí.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year. “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's an English translation of the Yoruba document, aiming for clarity and accuracy:

**WHO Calls for Innovative Solutions to Combat Malaria**

The World Health Organization (WHO) is providing funding for research and development of new technologies to combat malaria.  With the goal of achieving Zero Malaria by 2030, the WHO is investing significantly in the field of malaria control.  An estimated 1.5 million children in Africa – particularly in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi – die from malaria and other febrile illnesses each year.  The WHO has convened a high-level meeting to discuss the rollout of the RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S) vaccine, a novel, single-dose vaccine that the WHO is overseeing.  This vaccine, whose research was completed in 2019, is now being implemented to prevent malaria, particularly for children at high risk.  If deployed on a larger scale, the vaccine could protect up to 65% of children.

“We need innovative solutions to combat malaria,” stated Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. “These solutions, including effective tools for identifying and treating those at high risk, are crucial in many countries around the world.”

The 2023 World Malaria Day theme, “Accelerating Progress Towards Zero Malaria: Innovation, Coverage and Impact,” highlights the need for continued innovation, expanded coverage, and increased impact.  Within this theme, the WHO is urging the implementation of robust technologies and strategies to diagnose, train, and provide information on malaria.  Many countries are benefiting from this global effort.

translations.source: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ àìsàn ibà Lágbàyé tuntun, tí wọ́n gbé jáde ní oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2022, ìṣirò 247 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà tuntun ni ó wà ní ọdún 2021. Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ru ẹrù àìsàn náà tó pọ̀ jùlọ - tó dúró fún àpapọ 95% gbogbo àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà (234 mílíọ̀nù) àti 96% gbogbo ìṣẹ̀lẹ ikú (593 000) ní ọdún 2021. Bíi 80% àwọn ikú àìsàn ibà ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ló wà láàrin àwọn ọmọdé tí ọjọ́ orí wọn wà lábẹ́ ọdún márùn-ún. Àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ tuntun Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ. Pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan yìí, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìsàn ibà ò tíì ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí ó lè dènà, ṣàwarí kí ó sì tọ́jú àìsàn náà. Àwọn ìpèníjà nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà ti kenú, pàápàá jùlọ ní Ìwọ̀-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àkójọ àwọn rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn, àìsí ìpèsè owó, àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin tó lẹ, àti àdínkù nínú ìwúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìgbógun ti àìsàn ibà tòótọ́. Láti kojú àwọn ewu wọ̀nyìí àti láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò àìsàn ibà tó ní àfaradà gidi gan-an, WHO láìpé yìí ṣe àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tuntun àti àwòṣe, tó fi mọ́: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun láti kojú jáàgùn apa-àìsàn-ibà ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀; ìpilèṣẹ̀ tuntun láti dáwọ́ ìtànkálẹ̀ Anopheles stephensi dúró ní àwọn agbègbè ìlú;
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations. Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;

Approximately 80% of malaria deaths are found in children under five years old.

translations.source: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun, tí WHO àti UN-Habitat jọ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀, láti tọ́ àwọn adarí ìlú sọ́nà nínú ìdènà àìsàn ibà ní ìlú. irinṣẹ́ tuntun láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àyẹ̀wò ètò ìtọpinpin àìsàn ibà wọn àti láti mọ àwọn agbègbè fún ìdókówò WHO tún ti ṣe àfikún sí kíkó àkóyawọ́, àyípadà àti ànfààní sí àwọn àbá lórí àìsàn ibà rẹ̀. Àwọn Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún àìsàn ibà ti wà lórí àwọn ìkànnì àkósódù méjì: MAGICapp àti ohun èlò ìmúṣiṣẹ́ “Malaria Toolkit”. WHO ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe àwọn àbá náà ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ààyè ààrùn lábẹ́lé fún ipa tó pọ̀. Àwọn àfojúsọ́nà fún àwọn ìdásí tuntun Ìdókòwò onítẹ̀síwájú nínú àgbékalẹ̀ àti ìṣàmúlò àwọn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tuntun àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀ máa ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn àìsàn ibà àgbáyé ọdún 2030. Àjẹsára àìsàn ibà kejì, àjẹsára R21/Matrix-M (R21), tí ó bá gba àṣẹ, lè ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti dí àlàfo ńlá tó wà láàrin ìfiránṣẹ́ àti ìbéèrè kí ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti mú àdínkù bá àìlera àwọn ọmọdé àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà. Kàn-án-pá ló jẹ́ fún WHO láti tẹ̀síwájú àtúnyẹwò fínífíní àti ti akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó péye fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà R21 lọ́gán tí àlékún dátà ààbò pàtàkì àti ipa láti abala 3 ìdánwò R21 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ bá ti wà nílẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì pèsè rẹ̀ fún WHO. Ìpín sí ìsòrí àfihàn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà RTS,S ní àfikún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni ìrètí wà pé yóò bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2024.
translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact. Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.

“A new initiative, spearheaded by WHO and UN-Habitat, aims to provide concise urban data within cities. Innovative solutions are being developed to assist countries in strengthening their urban data systems and to identify priority areas. WHO has also expanded its support for data collection, providing training and funding to data professionals on urban data.  WHO’s Single Data Resources include two applications: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit.” WHO encourages countries to utilize these tools to generate data for greater impact.  Recommendations for future innovative digital health infrastructure within the initiative and the development of new vector control technologies are considered regardless of the need to report urban data by 2030. The R21/Matrix-M (R21) data mapping system, if adopted, can collaborate to eliminate the remaining gaps between funding and implementation to make data accessible to children and prevent deaths from urban data issues. WHO is committed to continuous and effective training and capacity building for the implementation of the R21 data mapping system, as the quality of data and its impact on reducing malaria by three doses of the RTS,S vaccine are crucial.  A pilot program for the expansion of the RTS,S vaccine data mapping in Sub-Saharan Africa is scheduled to begin in early 2024.”

translations.source: Ní ààyè ìdènà alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn ohun èlò 28 tuntun ló wà nínú ojú òpó R&D. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ tó wà lábẹ́ ìgbéléwọ̀n ni, fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ẹ̀yà tuntun àwọ̀n apẹ̀fọn, àwọn ìjẹ aṣàfojúsùn tí ó máa ń pe ẹ̀fọn, àwọn apanirun ní ààyè kan, àwọn atanipa ilé tó lágbára (àwọn túúbù Eaves) àti ṣíṣe àyípadà jínnì àwọn ẹ̀fọn. Àwọn oníwádìí ń mú ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú aláìní-ACT ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ní ẹ̀ka àwọn oògùn apa-àìsàn-ibà nítorí ìjẹyọ àti ìtànkálẹ̀ àwọn jáàgùn artemisinin. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn oògùn ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀, bíi "triple-ACTs," tí ó ń lo àpapọ artemisinin àti àwọn oògùn alájọṣepọ̀ méjì, ti wà lójú ọ̀nà láti mú àdínkù bá ewu jáàgùn. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Fún àlàyé síwájú sí i nípa ìpolongo Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé WHO, kàn sí https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

“In response to the malaria challenge, there are 28 new, innovative products in the R&D eye. The technologies available include, for example, new professional mosquito nets, digital health records that are called electronic health records, innovative drugs with a specific action, high-quality pharmaceutical products (such as Tube Eaves), and a large number of electronic records.  Researchers are developing tools to implement low-ACT regimens based on the use of artemisinin and other drugs for the treatment of malaria parasites.  The development of triple-ACTs, such as “triple-ACTs,” which use two artemisinin-based drugs in combination, is being pursued to reduce the risk of drug resistance in mosquitoes.  A link for the public: For more information about the World Malaria Day campaign of the WHO, visit https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023”

translations.source: Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA sún sí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè rẹ̀ Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ilé ìwòsan Ibùdó ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ kórajọ sí Cape Town láti jíròrò lórí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA àti ìgbaraẹni nímọ̀ràn. Ó lé ní 200 àwọn akópa ilẹ̀-òkèèrè tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni wọ́n pàdé ní Cape Town ní ọ̀sẹ̀ yìí fún ìpàdé ìfojúrinjú àkọ́kọ́ wọn. Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO), Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus àti Dr Joe Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera àti Mínísítà fún Kátà-kárà àti Ilé-iṣẹ́, Mr Ebrahim Patel ti orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ onípele-gíga láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń pèsè owó ni wọ́n darapọ̀ mọ́ wọn, ìpàdé aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yìí ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò ìlọsíwájú láti ìgbà tí WHO àti Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Òkudù ní ọdún 2021. Inú mi dùn láti wà ní Cape Town níbi pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ wa láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwòṣe alálòpẹ́ fún ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA láti fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì ní ànfààní adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera adóólà-ẹ̀mí mìíràn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ìwúrí ńlá gidi gan-an ni àṣeyọrí gbogbo àwọn tí wọ́n lọ́wọ́ sí iṣẹ́-àkànṣe yìí jẹ́ fún mi; ní nǹkan bí ọdún méjì dín a ti fi hàn pé a lè jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, a ṣe àṣeyọrí lápapọ̀." Àwọn tí wọ́n kópa nínú ìpàdé náà ni àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí láti orílẹ̀-èdè 15 nínú ètò náà, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó ń léwájú, ilé-iṣẹ́, àwọn aṣojú ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn olùpèsè owó. Ní àkókò ìpàdé ọlọ́jọ́-márùn náà, àwọn akópa máa sọ nípa ìlọsíwájú wọn ó sì jíròrò lórí àwọn aṣokùnfà pàtàkì fún ìtẹ̀síwájú Ètò náà bíi àwọn ìṣòro ohun-ìní ọlọgbọ́n àti àwọn tó jẹ́ ti òfin, bákan náà ni sáyẹ́nsì fún ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA àti àwọn ìṣàmúlò pàtàkì tó níṣe pẹ̀lú LMICs ní agbègbè àwọn ààrùn mìíràn bíi KASA àti ikọ́fe. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti fi hàn pé àìdọ́gba tó pọ̀ wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn ohun èlò ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn àjẹsára.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021. “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“The mRNA Research Consortium is making progress in collaboration with the Global Health Institute. The mRNA research hub is in Africa, specifically in South Africa. Partnerships with Cape Town are being established to facilitate progress on mRNA vaccine development and related issues. There are 200 skilled technicians working with the mRNA Research Consortium, who were recruited in Cape Town this year for advanced training. 

WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade, Industry and Competition of South Africa, jointly met with high-level officials from countries that have provided funding to them, to further strengthen global development until WHO and the Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) launched the research in November 2021.

I am pleased to be in Cape Town with our partners to conduct a detailed review of a pilot project for mRNA research to provide vaccines to countries with limited access and a double disadvantage in the face of the pandemic. WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus stated this. “It is extremely regrettable that all those who have assisted in this work are not recognized; in two years, we have planned to work together, but we have now jointly recognized it.”

Those participating in the meeting include partnerships being established with 15 countries through the consortium, including experts, institutions, corporate bodies, and donors. At the official ceremony, the fellows discussed their progress and facilitated dialogue on key issues for the adoption of the Consortium’s activities among long-term and established partners, particularly for mRNA research and relevant models with LMICs in the KASA and African regions.

The COVID-19 pandemic has shown a significant amount of inequality in the supply of vaccines, with a large number of vaccines lacking access. ”

translations.source: Títí di oṣù Èrenà ọdún 2023, ọdún mẹ́ta lé lẹ́yìn tí WHO kéde COVID-19 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Pàjáwírì Ìlera Àwùjọ fún Ẹ̀dun Ọkàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (PHEIC), 69.7% àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé ti gba ó kéré jù oògùn àjẹsára COVID-19 kan. Pàtàkì jùlọ, ìpín yìí ṣì wà lábẹ́ 30% ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré (LCIs). Ètò yìí gbèrò láti ṣe àfikún sí ànfààní adọ́gba sí àjẹsára mRNA pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfikún sí pínpín ìkápá ìpèsè alálòpẹ́ jákèjádò LMICs, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ ní ẹkùn agbègbè àti ẹlẹ́kùnjẹkùn, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti Ìrónilágbára fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ lábẹ́lé nípasẹ ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àkànṣe àti aláfibọ̀ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́. Nǹkan tó dá yátọ̀ nínú Àwòṣe Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni ìgbésẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi tí yóò fi ààyè sílẹ̀ fún pínpín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn tí yóò gbà á kí ó ba lè dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tó nílò rẹ̀ nípasẹ àwọn ìpèsè lábẹ́lé àti ẹkùn agbègbè kíákíá. Dr Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa, sọ wí pé: "Nǹkan tí a rí níbi lónì, jẹ́ àkókò kan nínú ìtàn, Ètò kan tí wọ́n gbèrò láti ró àwọn LMIC lágbára nípasẹ ojú òpó àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé. Inú mi dùn láti rí ìtẹ̀síwájú tí ó ti di ṣíṣe láàrín àkókò díẹ̀ mo sì gba àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọ̀dọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lóríṣiríṣi - àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè bíi South Africa tí ó ní ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa tó sì lágbára tí wọ́n sì ti ṣetán láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ láti ọ̀dọ ara wọn kí wọ́n sì tún sọ ọ́ fún ará wọn." Mínísítà Blade Nzimande fún Ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ Ilé-ìwé Gíga àti Ìmọ̀ Sáyẹ́nsì, sọ wí pé: Tiwa ni àfojúsùn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA tayọ COVID-19 nìkan. Àwọn ìkápá tí à ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ń wá láti ró wa lágbára láti kojú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ọjọ́ iwájú mìíràn tí àjẹsára wọn ò lè lo àwọn ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA kan náà. Ó jẹ́ àfojúsùn kan tí ó fi tẹjú rẹ̀ mọ́ àwọn ààrùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn agbègbè wa bíi àìsàn ibà, ikọ́fe àti KASA." Ìjókòó náà tún sààmì àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ilé Ìwòsàn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen ní Cape Town ní ojú Dr Tedros àti Dr Matshidoso Moeti Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Charles Gore Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún MPP àti Ọ̀narébù Mínísítà tí ó kópa nínú ayẹyẹ gígé ríbínì ní Afrigen.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network. I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis:
Okay, here’s a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

“As of June 2023, three years after WHO declared COVID-19 as a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of people who had contracted the COVID-19 virus had fully recovered. Notably, this rate is significantly higher in countries with low income levels (LCIs). The meeting was held to further develop strategies for using mRNA vaccines, alongside expanding access to a wide range of vaccines, particularly for low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), facilitating cooperation in regional and global forums, and establishing infrastructure and training for healthcare workers based on global standards and data, and supporting the work of regulatory agencies. 

A key concern within the mRNA Vaccine Innovation Hub is the increasing diversity of signals that could indicate a decline in the effectiveness of mRNA vaccines for various populations that may lose their ability to respond to them through antibodies generated by vaccines. Dr. Phaahla, Minister of Health in South Africa, stated: “What we see in the data, is a single story, a meeting that was held to help LMICs recover through the power of global cooperation. I am pleased to see the progress being made among several countries, including South Africa, which has a large, functioning, and increasingly effective vaccine ecosystem that has been scaled up to operate globally, and to share its knowledge with its own people.” Minister Blade Nzimande, Minister of Higher Education and Science and Technology, stated: “The rollout of the mRNA vaccine for COVID-19 is only one thing. The vaccines that are being developed are increasingly being found to be effective in helping to combat local outbreaks that cannot be addressed with existing mRNA vaccines. It is a concern that can be easily transmitted as data, knowledge, and KASA [a South African health initiative].” 

The meeting also expressed strong support for the mRNA Vaccine Innovation Hub in Cape Town, led by Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore, MPP, and a Minister who appeared in a live broadcast in Cape Town. ”

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Petro Terblanche, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Afrigen sọ wí pé: "Inú gbogbo ikọ̀ Afrigen dùn láti ṣe àṣeyọrí pàtàkì yìí pẹ̀lú píparí ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Ìkànnì yìí wà nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA láti ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin àti ìpèsè ilé ìwòsàn níbi tí àwọn olùdíje Ibùdó mRNA àjẹsára COVID-19 AfriVac 2121 ti wà ní abala ìfẹ̀lójú. Láti bíi oṣù 18 sẹ́yìn, Afrigen ti la àyípadà tó lápẹẹrẹ kọjá pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn ojú òpó àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn olùbánidámọ́ràn tí Ètò yìí fi ààyè gbà. A ti fi kún ìkápá àti ìkáátò wa láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn òdiwọ̀n ojúlówó tó ga jùlọ fún àgbékalẹ̀ àjẹsára mRNA, tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún èròngbà láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá alálòpẹ́ nínú LMICs láti pèsè àwọn àjẹsára mRNA ". Afrigen ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà ìpèsè àjẹsára COVID-19 ní àṣeyọrí pẹ̀lú òdiwọn ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀ tí ó sì ń ṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ìlànà yẹn dé ìpele tí ó ṣe é lò fún ìgbà pípẹ́ fún ìpèsè àwọn abala àjẹsára tí yóò jẹ́ lílo nínú Abala I/II àyẹ̀wò ajẹmọ́-ìtọ́jú sí àwọn òdiwọ̀n GMP. Ní ìlànà adọ́gba, Afrigen máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àti ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ojú òpó náà. Àwọn olùdásílẹ̀ náà kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó lápapọ̀ títí di òní ní US$ 177 mílíọ̀nù tí orílẹ̀-èdè France sì jẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ láti pèsè owó fún iṣẹ́ ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Àwọn tó péjú síbi ìpàdé náà ni àwọn aṣojú láti Àjọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù, Belgium, Germany bákan náà ni Norway, Canada, Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, South Africa, àti ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delaney, Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ GlobalAffairs Canada fún Gúúsù àti Ìlà-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ sọ wí pé: "Orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ń ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn rẹ̀ fún Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA inú wa sì dùn láti kéde ìtẹ̀síwájú àfikún 15 mílíọ̀nù dọ́là owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada níbi ìpàdé náà lónì. Èyí mú gbogbo owó tí orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ti fi dásí Ètò náà jẹ́ 45 mílíọ̀nù dólà owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada.
translations.reference: Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards. In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.
translations.hypothesis: Petro Terblanche, a leading figure for Afrigen, stated: “All the technology of Afrigen is ready to contribute to this important achievement only with the support of academic research mRNA. These technologies are within the framework of mRNA research platform for drug development and digital health at the level of mRNA vaccine development for the COVID-19 AfriVac 2121 by the European mRNA consortium. By September 18th, Afrigen has signed a preliminary agreement with the leading eye specialists of various organizations and stakeholders of this network that have been used to coordinate with global health organizations for the production of mRNA vaccines, which is working to build a platform for the production of a large number of mRNA vaccines in LMICs to develop vaccine technologies. Afrigen has built a COVID-19 vaccine development platform with a production cost for global health research that is also being finalized to the level that it is used for immediate use for vaccine production of vials of mRNA that will be useful in Phase I/II clinical trials for collaborative decision-making with GMP manufacturers. In the development process, Afrigen will undertake academic and research collaboration for these specialists. These specialists play a significant role in the successful operation of this network with a combined amount of up to US$177 million from the French government to support research collaboration for mRNA vaccines. Supporters of this initiative include the European Union, Belgium, Germany, Norway, Canada, the Global Fund, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delaney, GlobalAffairs Canada Director for the United Nations and Canada, stated: “Canada is investing in this mRNA research network and is ready to contribute up to 15 million Canadian dollars to this initiative. This makes the total amount that Canada has invested in this network 45 million Canadian dollars.”

"EC ti dá €40 mílíọ̀nù tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ó sì ti buwọ́lù owó ìrànwọ́ láìpẹ́ yìí pẹ̀lu EIB fún €15,5 mílíọ̀nù láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfẹ̀lójú ìkápá ìpèsè àwọn àjẹsára. Èyí jẹ́ lára àpapọ ìdókówò lábẹ́ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ EU Global Gateway, níbi tí wọ́n ti ṣe àkójọ €1 bílíọ̀nù lé lábẹ́ Ikọ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù lórí Ìpèsè àwọn ohun èlò ìlera. Ó ti wá ṣe pàtàkì pàápàá báyìí láti kojú àwọn ìfúnpinpin òfin àti ìbéèrè tó ṣe é ṣe, láti rí i dájú pé kìí ṣe ìpèsè àjẹsára tí wọ́n jẹ́ ojúlówó tó dára jùlọ ní gbogbo ẹkùn agbègbè àgbáyé nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n pẹ̀lu ànfààní ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára wọ̀nyẹn."

translations.source: Àwọn ìyàn sípò aṣíwájú pàtàkì tí wọ́n ṣe láti darí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìdarí àti ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ WHO Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ti ṣe ìyànsípò àwọn lọ́gà-lọ́gà márùn-ún tuntun sí ikò adarí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva. Àwọn ìyànsípò tuntun náà tẹ̀lé ìtúnni yàn sípò Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus sí sáà ọlọ́dún-márùn-ún kejì gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀. Àpapọ ikọ̀ àwọn olùdarí náà ni wọ́n ti ṣe láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn ohun pàtàkì Àjọ náà fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ tí wọn ó sì ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí àti èròngbà àyípadà àlá WHO. Láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú yá lórí ìṣàmúlò Ètò Àpapọ̀ fún Iṣẹ́ WHO 13th (GPW13), àti àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn "bílíọ̀nù mẹ́ta" rẹ̀ àti Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera náà, iṣẹ́ WHO fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ dá lórí àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì márùn-ún: ṣíṣe àyípadà iṣẹ́ lọ sí ibi ìpolongo ìlera àti ìdènà ààrùn pẹ̀lú kíkojú àwọn aṣokùnfà ìpìlẹ̀ rẹ̀; ṣíṣe àtúntò àwọn ètò ìlera fún ètò ìlera alábọ́dé láti ìpìlẹ̀; Ríró àwọn ètò náà lágbára fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí pàjáwírì ìlera; ṣíṣe àmúlò agbára sáyẹ́nsì, ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, dátà àti àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ilera; àti ríró WHO lágbára gẹ́gẹ́ bíi aláṣẹ tó ń léwájú lórí ìlera àgbáyé àti láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ipa ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè.
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries.

“Special commemorative events were held to present the WHO’s global health strategy and leadership to the WHO Executive Board.  These new leadership appointments were made by Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus as the Chief of Staff.  These new leaders were appointed to inform the public about the WHO’s priorities for the coming years, working together with the Chief of Staff to promote awareness of these priorities and to facilitate their implementation.  To support the launch of the 13th Global Programme of Work (GPW13) for WHO and its three-pillar strategy and the High-Level Independent Panel, WHO’s work for the coming years will focus on key priorities:  establishing a global health observatory;  strengthening health networks for countries;  and ensuring the availability of health data and expertise to enable health decision-making.  Furthermore, WHO is recognized as a leading force in global health and contributes to the endorsement of its role in countries.”
